---
title: "Untitled"
author: "Skylar Zeng"
date: "2026-03-18"
output: html_document
---

Modular SNV Distance Pipeline Design

This document outlines a modular R pipeline to compute core, soft‑core and non‑core
single nucleotide variant (SNV) distances from whole‑genome alignments.  It
summarises the biological assumptions, computational steps and recommended
parameters, and proposes functions implemented in the accompanying
snv_pipeline_skeleton.R script.

Biological definitions and assumptions
	•	Core sites – positions where all genomes in the alignment carry a
valid unambiguous base.  Strict core filtering discards any site that has a
missing value in any genome.  As described in the Core‑SNP‑filter tool,
invariant sites (positions with zero or one unique unambiguous base) are
treated separately from core/non‑core status ￼.
	•	Soft‑core sites – positions retained if a specified proportion of genomes
have valid bases.  Recent work demonstrates that allowing a small amount of
missing data (for example retaining sites where ≥95 % of genomes have
unambiguous bases) preserves many more informative sites than a strict core
filter ￼.  The proportion threshold should be user
adjustable; by default the pipeline allows up to 10 % missingness (i.e. a
90 % core).
	•	Non‑core distances – distances calculated without global site filtering,
comparing each pair of genomes across only the positions where both genomes have
data.  Pairwise deletion of missing sites is therefore required.
	•	Missing data – the characters N, n, -, ?, . and other
ambiguous codes are treated as missing.  The pipeline allows this set to be
customised.
	•	Genome quality – genomes with excessive missingness should be removed
before computing distances.  Studies using whole‑genome alignments have
excluded genomes with more than 10 % ambiguous bases ￼, so
10 % missingness is used as the default genome‑level filter.  Users can adjust
this cutoff and inspect the distribution of genome missingness to choose
appropriate thresholds.
	•	Distance model – pairwise distances are computed with ape::dist.dna
using the “N” model, which returns the number of differing sites between
sequences.  For core and soft‑core distances, sites with missing data are
removed globally; for non‑core distances, pairwise deletion of missing data
ensures comparisons use only overlapping valid sites ￼.

Pipeline overview

The pipeline can be divided into three major sections:
	1.	Pre‑Gubbins alignment QC and distances
	2.	Recombination masking summaries (Gubbins outputs)
	3.	Post‑Gubbins alignment QC and distances

An optional step masks positions in the post‑Gubbins alignment that had any
missingness in the pre‑alignment.  Each section performs similar tasks:
	•	read alignments;
	•	compute genome‑ and site‑level missingness;
	•	filter genomes on a missingness threshold;
	•	identify core and soft‑core sites for multiple thresholds;
	•	calculate core, soft‑core and non‑core SNV distances;
	•	summarise and visualise missingness, retained sites and distance distributions;
	•	record the number of genomes and positions retained at each step.

Recommended workflow
	1.	Read pre‑Gubbins alignment using read_alignment(), which returns
DNAStringSet and DNAbin representations.
	2.	Compute a valid‑position matrix with valid_position_matrix(), marking
positions that are non‑missing for each genome.
	3.	Summarise the alignment via summarise_alignment(), reporting the
number of genomes, alignment length, number of invariant and variable sites,
and overall missingness.
	4.	Assess missingness:
	•	genome_missingness() returns the number and proportion of missing positions
per genome.  Plot the distribution with plot_genome_missingness() to
identify outliers.  Remove genomes exceeding the chosen missingness
threshold via filter_genomes().
	•	site_missingness() computes missingness per site.  Plot using
plot_site_missingness() to visualise highly missing positions.
	5.	Define core and soft‑core sites using identify_core_sites() with
different thresholds (e.g. 1.0 for strict core; 0.9 for a 90 % core) and
generate a retention curve with soft_core_retention() and
plot_soft_core_retention() to illustrate how site counts change with
decreasing core thresholds ￼.
	6.	Compute SNV distances with compute_snv_distances().  For core and
soft‑core distances, supply the logical vector of retained sites.  For
non‑core distances, set the core_sites argument to NULL to enable
pairwise deletion of missing sites.  Convert to long format using
distance_matrix_to_long() and summarise with summarise_distances().
	7.	Recombination masking summaries (optional): use summarise_masking() to
count the number and proportion of positions masked by Gubbins and, if
possible, summarise block lengths.  Visualise the location of masked blocks
along the genome using an appropriate plotting function (to be developed).
	8.	Mask post‑Gubbins alignment positions based on missingness in the
pre‑alignment with mask_post_based_on_pre().  This ensures that post‑core
sites are a subset of pre‑core sites when desired.
	9.	Repeat steps 1–6 for the post‑Gubbins alignment, recording the number of
genomes and positions retained and summarising the new distance matrices.
	10.	Cross‑step comparison: compile tables comparing pre‑ and post‑Gubbins
results, including numbers of retained genomes/sites, proportions of
missingness, retained variant sites, and statistics of pairwise distances.
Visualise distributions of distances across methods using
plot_distance_distributions().
	11.	Final outputs: organise all tables, figures and log files in a
structured output directory.  Optionally generate a flowchart summarising
the numbers of genomes and positions at each stage and implement an
RMarkdown report to document the run.

Implementation notes

The provided snv_pipeline_skeleton.R script implements the core analytical
functions described above.  These functions are deliberately modular; they do
not read from or write to disk, plot figures, or parse command line
arguments.  A separate wrapper script should:
	•	parse user‑supplied parameters (input file paths, missingness thresholds,
core thresholds, output directories, options to mask post‑alignment etc.);
	•	call the functions in the appropriate order for pre‑ and post‑Gubbins
alignments;
	•	generate plots and write tables/figures to disk with informative file names;
	•	produce log messages detailing the parameters used and the number of
genomes/sites retained at each step;
	•	integrate with a SLURM batch script to run on high‑performance computing
clusters.

Handling of missing data

Missing characters are defined by default as N, n, -, ?, . and
ambiguous codes such as X.  Users may customise this set if, for example,
lower‑case bases should be treated differently.  The function
valid_position_matrix() generates a logical matrix indicating where bases are
present, which is used throughout the pipeline.

Genome filtering

Filtering genomes with more than a specified proportion of missing data helps
avoid artifacts from low‑quality sequences.  The default threshold is 10 %
missingness in line with published analyses of bacterial genomes ￼.
filter_genomes() returns both the filtered alignment and the identifiers of
removed genomes so they can be reported in QC summaries.

Core and soft‑core definitions

The pipeline implements core and soft‑core definitions following the
Core‑SNP‑filter logic: a site is retained if the proportion of genomes with a
valid base at that site exceeds a user‑defined threshold ￼.  All
non‑A/C/G/T characters are treated identically.  Soft‑core thresholds can be
swept across a range (e.g. 1.0 down to 0.8) to assess the trade‑off between
missingness tolerance and alignment length ￼.

Distance calculation

Pairwise SNV distances are calculated using the ape::dist.dna function with
the “N” model.  This returns the number of differing sites between sequences,
and the pairwise.deletion argument controls how missing data are handled
￼.  For core and soft‑core alignments, pairwise.deletion is
set to FALSE because all missing data have been removed by site filtering.
For non‑core distances, pairwise.deletion is set to TRUE so that each
pairwise comparison uses only overlapping positions.

Recombination masking

To summarise recombination masking, the pipeline includes a generic
summarise_masking() function that reads a list of masked positions and
reports how many sites are masked and what proportion of the genome this
represents.  If start–end ranges are provided, the function also returns the
lengths of masked blocks.  Custom parsing may be needed for different
Gubbins output formats.

Post‑alignment masking

The optional mask_post_based_on_pre() function replicates the behaviour of
manual masking scripts: it replaces positions in the post‑Gubbins alignment
with N wherever any genome in the pre‑alignment had missing data.  This
ensures that post‑Gubbins core/soft‑core analyses consider only positions that
were unambiguous in the pre‑alignment.

Next steps
	•	Implement a command‑line interface (CLI) or configuration file to drive the
pipeline, allowing users to specify input files, thresholds and options.
	•	Add detailed plotting functions for recombination masking and cross‑step
comparisons.
	•	Produce an RMarkdown report template that assembles the QC summaries,
figures and distance tables into a human‑readable report.
	•	Integrate the pipeline into a SLURM batch script to run on a cluster,
capturing logs and outputs in a systematic fashion.

By structuring the pipeline around modular functions and explicit definitions,
users can confidently compute SNV distances while preserving information and
identifying potential artifacts introduced by filtering and masking.
